State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

February 15, 1984


J. Steven Burris
Deputy County Counsel
700 H Street, Suite 2650
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice
                                            Our File No. A-83-279

Dear Mr. Burris:

     This letter is sent in response to your request for advice
from this office on behalf of F. Susan Harrison, the Deputy
Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports,
concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.1/

                          FACTUAL BACKGROUND2/

     Ms. Harrison's husband is employed as a pilot by Western
Airlines. As a benefit of his job, Ms. Harrison and her husband
receive discounted airline travel on Western and other
airlines. In addition, her husband participates in an employee
stock sharing program.

     Ms. Harrison's job duties as the Deputy Director for Finance
and Administration include:

          1.   Preparing the Department's budget.

          2.   Analyzing and recommending changes to airport rates
and charges (e.g., rates for T-hangars and tie-downs).

_________________
1/  California Government Code Sections 81000-91014.  All
statutory references are to the California Government Code.

2/  These facts come from our telephone conversations,
Ms. Harrison's memo to your office dated June 9, 1983, your
letters to me dated December 13, 1983 and January 9, 1984, and
the materials included with your letter.

J. Steven Burris
February 15, 1984
Page 2


     3.  Negotiating tenant leases at both Sacramento
Metropolitan and Executive Airports.

     4.  Administering the air trade development program
which is a marketing program designed to promote use of
Metro Airport.

     My understanding is that you have already advised
Ms. Harrison with respect to the setting of airport rates and
charges. Your specific question to us is whether Ms. Harrison
would have a conflict of interest in negotiating the airline
leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport.3/

     The County of Sacramento currently has an "Airline Operating
Agreement and Terminal Building Lease" (the "Agreement") with
the airlines for use of Sacramento Metropolitan Airport ("Metro
Airport"). This Agreement was negotiated by Ms. Harrison's
predecessor and became effective in June 1983; it is a five-year
agreement. The negotiations for the Agreement are conducted
between the Deputy Director and a group of airline
representatives. The County is required to treat the airlines
in an identical manner.4/

     The County policy on the operation of Metro Airport is that
it must be self-supporting. Accordingly, in negotiating the
Agreement, the Deputy Director (Ms. Harrison) begins with the
total cost of the airport operation, subtracts the amounts from
revenue sources other than the airlines,5/ and arrives at the
amount which must be recovered from the airlines. The airlines
are charged for their use of the airport in two ways. One
charge is the landing strip usage fee which is set at a certain

_____________
     3/  Both of these questions raise the issue of the
possible application of Section 1090. This office renders
advice only on the provisions of the Political Reform Act;
questions concerning Section 1090 should be directed to the
Attorney General's office.

     4/  The County receives aid from the Airport Development
Air Program under the jurisdiction of the Federal Aviation
Administration. A condition on receiving this aid is that all
of the carriers must be treated alike in operation and lease
agreements.

     5/  Other revenue sources include the hotel and gift shop,
car rental and taxi concessions and other miscellaneous sources.

J. Steven Burris
February 15, 1984
Page 3


cost per ton of landing weight utilized by the airline. The
other charge is a cost per square foot of space used by the
airline in the terminal. The total which is charged the
airlines must add up with the other revenues to the amount
originally set as the total cost for airport operations. Even
though this formula is used, there is still discretion in the
setting of the charges and in spelling out the other terms of
the Agreement.

In addition to participating in the negotiations for the
Agreement itself, the Deputy Director is responsible for annual
modifications of the Agreement. These modifications to the
charges are done pursuant to a formula in the Agreement.

ANALYSIS

As you know, Section 87100 prohibits a public official from
making or participating in governmental decisions in which the
official has a financial interest in the outcome. Section 87103
defines a financial interest as a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(Section 87103(a)-(d).)

Investments include investments of a spouse, and income includes
income to a spouse. Sections 82030 and 82034. Thus, Western
Airlines is a source of income to Ms. Harrison and a business

J. Steven Burris
February 15, 1984
Page 4


entity in which she has an investment. Accordingly,
Ms. Harrison may not make or participate in decisions which
could have a material financial effect on Western Airlines,
distinguishable from their effect on the public generally.

     It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the
airline leases at Metro Airport will have a financial effect on
the airlines. In addition, it is clear that the effects of
leases on airport tenants are distinguishable from their effects
on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. In
particular, decisions concerning the County's agreement with the
tenant airlines will primarily affect only the airlines
involved. Thus the key question for analysis is whether
modifications of the existing agreement with the airlines and/or
the new agreement to be negotiated will have a material effect
on Western Airlines.

     The Commission has provided numerical guidelines for the
determination of a material effect on a business entity:

                              * * *

          (C)   Current assets or liabilities by the lessor
of:
               1.   One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);

               or

               2.   One half of one percent if the effect is
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

          Current assets are deemed to be decreased by
the amount of any expenses incurred as a result of
a governmental decision.

                    (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                    18702(b)(1)(C).)&/   (Copy
                    enclosed.)

     Assuming that Western Airlines' annualized gross revenues
exceed $1 million, a financial effect would have to be $100,000
to be considered material.  You indicated to me that Western
Airlines pays approximately $50,000 to the County for its use of
Metro Airport under the current Agreement.

_____________________

&/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A) and (B) are
not relevant to this situation.

J. Steven Burris
February 15, 1984
Page 5


     It does not appear that any modifications of the existing
Agreement could have a $100,000, or material, effect on Western
Airlines. The modifications would only adjust the fees paid by
the airlines to compensate for inflation and other changes in
the cost and revenue picture. Thus, Ms. Harrison can
participate in decisions concerning modifications of the present
Agreement.

     It is a more difficult question whether Ms. Harrison will be
able to participate in the negotiations for a new Agreement when
this Agreement expires. Assuming that Western Airlines plans to
continue doing business in Sacramento, it would first be
necessary to determine what issues were on the negotiating
table, and whether the impact on Western Airlines from the
potential resolutions to any of these issues would be
significant. We understand that at the outset of negotiations
it may be difficult to determine what the issues will be. Based
on her knowledge and experience, Ms. Harrison should first
determine what serious proposals from the County and from the
airlines will probably be on the negotiating table. Then, for
each proposal, if either the difference between the existing
Agreement and the proposal or the differences between the
proposals on the same issue on the table amount to a possible
$100,000 effect on Western Airlines, then Ms. Harrison should
not participate in, at the minimum, the negotiations on that
proposal.1/ For example, if the airlines propose what would
amount to a $50,000 reduction in fees and charges paid by
Western Airlines, and the County proposes a similar increase in
the amounts paid, the range between the proposals adds up to
$100,000, and the decision on that issue would have a material
financial effect on Western Airlines. Possible negotiating
issues that could affect Western Airlines include not only fees
and other direct charges but also other contract provisions
(e.g., the amount of space allotted for the airline).

     In any event, the negotiations are a few years off, and
Ms. Harrison may wish to request our advice again when the
potential issues in the negotiation for the new Agreement become
clearer. It may be that there are no issues with respect to the
new Agreement that could have a $100,000 effect on Western
Airlines, and Ms. Harrison's disqualification would not be
required.

_______________
     1/ It may be that the proposal is at the center of the
negotiations, and, as a practical matter, she may have to
disqualify herself from the entire process.

J. Steven Burris
February 15, 1984
Page 6


     Please feel free to contact me if you would like to discuss
this letter or if you have any further questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maur: Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO

OFFICE OF THE COUNTY COUNSEL
700 H Street, Suite 2650
Sacramento, CA 95814
Phone: (916) 440-5544

Supervising Deputy County Counsel
Thomas A Daring
Robert J Stames
Darren Dougherty Jr
Melvin L Pryor
Marwyn D Price

Deputy County Counsel
Richard D Valker
J Steven Burns
Thomas M Case
William H Fowler
Elaine P DiPerna
Frank M Garcia
Manuel E Lopes
Loy C Prawney
Margaret L Hagerty
Anthony L Wright
Jerry Sterner
Paul J Ferrini
Edward G Liata
Jeff Davis
Kathleen A O'Connor
Derreck C Bell
Randona A Armstead
Paul Masterson

L. B. Blam
County Counsel

Fred C. Williams
Assistant County Counsel

January 9, 1984

Diane Maura Fishburn
Legal Division
Fair Political Practice Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your File No. A-83-279

Dear Mrs. Fishburn:

This is to respond to your questions regarding the discretion which is available to F. Susan Harrison, the Deputy Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports in negotiating County airline leases. It is the policy of the County to operate its airport in a self-supporting manner. To this end, the County negotiates with the airlines to recover its airport costs of operation and maintenance, capital expenditures, and reserves for capital expenditures.

These negotiations are conducted openly between the Deputy Director and a group consisting of representatives from each of the airlines. Beginning with the total cost of operation of the airport revenue sources are subtracted and the remaining cost is recovered from the airlines Revenue sources under the agreement consist of income which the County receives from sources such as the operation of the hotel and gift shop, car rental concessions, taxi concessions, and other miscellaneous sources of income. See copy of agreement attached. The remaining cost is recovered from the airlines under two fee categories. The first source of cost recovery is the landing strip usage fees which are set at a certain cost per ton of landing weight which is utilized by the airline. The second source of funds is the amount of space utilized by the airline; fees are charged for such space within the terminal on a per square foot basis. The total amount of such fees plus the other income sources must add up to the amount originally set for the total cost of operation of the airport.

Diane Maura Fishburn                -2-                January 9, 1983

     The discretion available to the Deputy Director in
negotiating the agreement is quite limited.  Under various agree-
ments the County has applied for and recieved aid from the
Airport Development Aid Program under the jurisdiction of the
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA).  As a result, the County
is bound under contract with the FAA to comply with Sponsors
Assurances.  Assurance No. 18, copy attached provides that the
carriers, including Western Airlines, shall be treated in an
identical manner.  The Deputy Director has no discretion to treat
Western Airlines any differently than any of the other airlines.
The discretion which is given to the deputy Director in such
negotiations is the ability to set the rate for landing fees on a
tonnage basis and the ability to set the price of the rental
space on a per square foot basis.  It should be noted that
although the existing agreement is for a five year period, the
agreement is nevertheless subject to modifications on an annual
basis.  Such modifications allow for little discretion on the
part of the Deputy Director as they are narrowly spelled out in a
formula in the agreement.

     Also enclosed for your information is the Attorney General
opinion dated May 30, 1975 and the letter requesting advice dated
June 9, 1983.

     If I can be of further assistance, please let me know.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        L. B. ELAM, County Counsel


                                        By    [signature]
                                             J. STEVEN BURRIS, Deputy

Enclosures

COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
CALIFORNIA

OFF AGENDA

June 8, 1977

TO: Board of Supervisors

FROM: Department of Airports

SUBJECT: METRO - DEVELOPMENT - ACCEPTANCE OF AIRPORT DEVELOPMENT GRANT
OFFER

On August 30, 1976, the Board of Supervisors authorized the Department
of Airports to prepare and submit a project application for Airport
Development Aid Program (ADAP) funding with respect to land acquisition
reimbursement and safety and security improvements at Sacramento Metro
Airport.

Under letter dated May 20, 1977, the Federal Aviation Administration
has advised that the final portion of the previously submitted project
application has been approved. Pursuant to FAA approval of the County's
application, a Grant offer has been submitted to Sacramento County in
the amount of $1,602,847.

The format of the offer is similar in content to those previously
accepted by the Board with certain conditions incorporated into the
Grant offer. A complete copy of the Grant offer and resolution au-
thorizing the Chairman of the Board to execute same is attached.

County Counsel has reviewed the Grant offer and finds it to be accep-
table in form.

It is the recommendation of the Department of Airports that the Board
of Supervisors:

1. Accept the Grant offer in the amount of $1,602,847 as
   submitted by the Federal Aviation Administration for
   ADAP funding at Sacramento Metro;

2. Approve the Resolution authorizing the Chairman to ex-
   ecute the Grant offer and supporting documents; and

3. Direct the Department of Airports and other County
   agencies as may be necessary, to comply with the con-
   ditions set forth in the subject Grant offer.

Respectfully submitted,

George W. McLaughlin
Director of Airports

Recommend Approval:

DAVID L. SMITH
County Executive

Attachment
cc: County Executive
    Assistant County Executive - Admin.
    County Counsel

Department of Airports (3)
Department of Public Works
Auditor

Page 9 of 14 pages

The Sponsor will disclose and make information about the contracts,
contracting procedures and requirements available to the designated
OMBE representative and minority firms on the same basis that such
information is disclosed and made available to other organizations
or firms. Responses by minority firms to invitations for bids
shall be treated in the same manner as all other responses to the
invitations for bids.

Compliance with the preceding paragraph will be deemed to constitute
compliance by the Sponsor with requirements of 49 CFR 21 Appendix
C(A)(1)(X), Regulations of the Office of the Secretary of Transportation.

10. It is understood and agreed that the Sponsor will provide for FAA
employees adequate parking accommodations satisfactory to the
Administrator at all FAA technical facilities (Air Navigation and
Air Traffic Control facilities) located on the airport. It is
further understood and agreed that Sponsor will provide, without
cost, adequate land for the purpose of parking all official vehicles
of the FAA (Government and privately owned when used for FAA business)
necessary for the maintenance and operation of the FAA facilities on
the airport. Such land shall be adjacent to the facilities served.

11. It is understood and agreed that the Sponsor shall comply with
the revised Administration requirements to FAR Part 152 as set
forth in the May 31, 1974, issue of the Federal Register, Volume
39, No. 106. It is further agreed that the Sponsor shall comply
with the assurances contained in FAA Form 5100-100 (or FAA Form
5100-101 as appropriate).

12. It is understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that
pursuant to FAR Part 152, Section 152.64, the FAA may by written
notice terminate or suspend this Grant in whole or in part, or
withhold payment if it finds that the Sponsor has failed to comply
with the conditions of the Grant or if it finds that continuation
of the project would not produce beneficial results.

13. It is understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that
the Sponsor shall comply with the reporting requirements set forth
in FAR Part 152, Section 152.66.

14. Assurance Number 18 of Part V of the project application incorporated
herein is amended by including at the end of the second sentence the
following language:

"Including the requirement that (A) each air carrier,
authorized to engage directly in air transportation
pursuant to Section 401 or 402 of the Federal Aviation

Standard Special Condition - Form Page f (3/77)

Page 10of14 pages

Act of 1958, using such airport shall be subject to
nondiscriminatory and substantially comparable rates,
fees, rentals, and other charges and nondiscriminatory
and substantially comparable rules, regulations, and
conditions as are applicable to all such air carriers
which make similar use of such airport and which utilize
similar facilities, subject to reasonable classifications
such as tenants or nontenants, and combined passenger and
cargo flights or all cargo flights, and such classification
or status as tenant or sponsor shall not be unreasonably withheld by
any sponsor provided an air carrier assumes obligations
substantially similar to those already imposed on tenant
air carriers, and (B) each fixed-base operator using a
general aviation airport shall be subject to the same
rates, fees, rentals, and other charges as are uniformly
applicable to all other fixed-base operators making the
same or similar uses of such airport utilizing the same
or similar facilities; provision (A) above, shall not
require the reformation of any lease or other contract
entered into by a sponsor before July 12, 1976; Provision (B)
above, shall not require the reformation of any lease
or other contract entered into by a sponsor before
July 1, 1975."

15. It is understood and agreed that no part of the Federal share of
an airport development project for which a grant is made under
the Airport and Airway Development Act of 1970, as amended (49 U.S.C.
1701 et seq.), or under the Federal Airport Act, as amended (49
U.S.C. 1101 et seq.), shall be included in the rate base in estab-
lishing fees, rates, and charges for users of the airport.

16. This project and all work performed thereunder is subject to the
Clean Air Act and the Federal Water Pollution Control Act. Accor-
dingly,

(a) The sponsor hereby stipulates that any facility to be utilized
in performance under the grant or to benefit from the grant
is not listed on the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
List of Violating Facilities.

(b) The sponsor agrees to comply with all the requirements of
Section 114 of the Clean Air Act and Section 308 of the Federal
Water Pollution Control Act and all regulations issued thera-
under.

(c) The sponsor shall notify the FAA of the receipt of any communi-
cation from the EPA indicating that a facility to be utilized
for performance of or benefit from the grant is under considera-
tion to be listed on the EPA List of Violating Facilities.

Standard Special Condition - Form Page 9 (1/77)

COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
Inter-Department Correspondence

RECEIVED
JUN 10 1983
COUNTY COUNSEL

Date June 9, 1983

To : Lee Elam
County Counsel

From : F. Susan Harrison
Deputy Director, Department of Airports

Subject : REQUEST FOR REVIEW OF CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATUS

As previously discussed with you, I would appreciate your review of
my duties with the Department of Airports to determine if there is
any conflict of interest with some of my personal interests. Those
areas of my personal life that have any connection whatsoever with
the department are described below. Likewise, I have outlined my
new duties as Deputy Director for Finance and Administration.

PERSONAL

1. My husband is employed as a pilot with Western Airlines. He
currently flies as a co-pilot on the Boeing 737 and is based
in Salt Lake City. He commutes to work in Salt Lake City
from Sacramento on Western. Strictly as a benefit of his job,
he and I and his parents receive discounted travel on Western
and on other airlines with whom Western has reciprocal travel
agreements. All Western employees and their families receive
the same travel benefits. I have not and would not accept free
or discounted travel from any airline as a result of my position
with the department.

2. My husband and I own two aircraft. One of these is stored in
one of the 151 County-owned T-hangars at Executive Airport,
for which we pay the going monthly rental.

3. We purchase fuel for our aircraft from the County.

4. We purchase some supplies from and have some services performed
on or for our aircraft by tenants at Executive Airport.

5. We live in a community called Fullertown, which is an airport
housing area on the east side of Executive Airport off 21st Street.
The several homes within the Fullertown community have direct taxi-
way access to Executive Airport. The community was established at
least fifteen years ago. We purchased an existing home there
with existing taxiway almost five years ago. A former partner in
our second aircraft built a home and attached hangar in Fullertown
about 5-1/2 years ago. He stores our second airplane in his
hangar.

. .

COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
OFFICE OF THE COUNTY COUNSEL
700 H Street, Suite 2650
Sacramento, CA 95814
Phone: (916) 440-5544

Supervising Deputy County Counsel
Thomas A Daring
Robert J Phelps
Clement J Dougherty
Monie L Strum
Merwin W Price

Deputy County Counsel
John P Conlin
J Sever Burns
Lawrence S James
William H Fowler
Elaine P DiPietro
Frank M Garcia
Manuel E Lopes
Lilly C Frawley
Margaret L Hagerty
Anthony L Wright
Barry Siemer
Richard Scheidt
Richard G Lara
John H Dodds
Kathleen A O'Connor
Denis J Zietf
Ramona A Armistead
Paul Reyiaga

L. B. Elam
County Counsel

Fred C. Williams
Assistant County Counsel

December 13, 1983

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Barbara A. Milman,
General Counsel

Re: Request for Conflict of Interest Opinion

Dear Ms. Milman:

This letter is written to request your opinion regarding a
potential conflict of interest which has arisen regarding the
employment of the Deputy Director of Airports of Sacramento
County.

The husband of the Deputy Director is employed as a pilot for
Western Airlines. In addition to his salary he receives benefits
such as discounted travel for his family on Western and other
airlines and stock ownership in Western Airlines.

The Deputy Director of Airports is responsible for the
negotiation of tenant leases at both Sacramento Executive and the
Sacramento Metropolitan Airports. A new five-year airline lease
negotiated by the predecessor of the present Deputy Director has
recently gone into effect. Such lease includes formulas for
annual adjustments to the terminal space rental rates and landing
fees. All signatory airlines (Western Airlines is a signatory
airline) pay the same rates. When the present lease expires, the
Deputy Director will be responsible for negotiating new leases
with the signatory airlines for recommendation to the Director of
Airports who will in turn recommend the lease for adoption by
the Board of Supervisors.

The negotiations with Western Airlines cannot be conducted
separately from negotiations with the other airlines because all

Fair Political
Practices Commission

-2-

December 13, 1983

the airlines are treated in an identical manner. The effect of
the negotiations is to set charges on all airlines for the use of
the County facility. The amounts of the charges and time over
which the charges are made are subject to negotiation. Western
Airlines' share of such charges will likely continue to be in
excess of $50,000.00 on an annual basis.

Our question is whether the Deputy Director must be
disqualified from negotiating the new leases with the signatory
airlines. If you need further clarifications or additional
information, please let me know.

Very truly yours,

L. B. ELAM
County Counsel

By
J. STEVEN BURRIS
Deputy County Counsel

sections.analysis:
As you know, Section 87100 prohibits a public official from· 
making or participating in governmental decisions in which the 
official has a financial interest in the outcome. 
Section 87103 
defines a financial interest as a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103 (a) - (d) .) 
Investments include investments of a spouse, and income includes 
income to a spouse. 
Sections 82030 and 82034. !Thus, Western 
Airlines is a source of income to Ms. Harrison and a business 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984  
entity in which she has an investment. 
Accordingly, 
Ms. Harrison may not make or participate in decisions which 
could have a material financial effect on Western Airlines, 
distinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the 
airline leases at Metro Airport will have a financial effect on 
the airlines. 
In addition, it is clear that the effects of 
leases on airport tenants are distinguishable from their effects 
on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. 
In 
particular, decisions concerning the County's agreement with the 
tenant airlines will primarily affect only the airlines 
involved. 
Thus the key question for analysis is whether 
modifications of the existing agreement with the airlines and/or 
the new agreement to be negotiated will have a material effect 
on Western Airlines. 
The Commission has provided numerical guidelines for the 
determination of a material effect on a business entity: 
of:
sections.question_synthetic: Whether F. Susan Harrison, Deputy Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports, has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when negotiating airline operating agreements at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport, given her husband's employment by Western Airlines and stock holdings in the company.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ms. Harrison may participate in routine modifications of the existing airline agreement since they would not have a material ($100,000+) financial effect on Western Airlines. However, for future negotiations of a new agreement, she must evaluate each proposal to determine if it could have a $100,000 or greater impact on Western Airlines; if so, she must disqualify herself from those specific negotiations.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr, old; LLM: Clear advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. Includes factual background, analysis of Political Reform Act provisions (Sections 87100, 87103), and materiality guidelines from administrative code. Secondary document included is incoming request letter from County Counsel dated January 9, 1984.

QUESTION: s concerning Section 1090 should be directed to the 
Attorney General's office. 
i/ The County receives aid from the Airport Development 
Air Program under the jurisdiction of the Federal Aviation 
Administration. 
A condition on receiving this aid is that all 
of the carriers must be treated alike in operation and lease 
agreements. 
i/ Other revenue sources include the hotel ,and gift shop, 
car rental and taxi concessions and other miscellaneous sources. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984

The 
other charge is a cost per square foot of space used by the 
airline in the terminal. 
The total which is charged the 
airlines must add up with the other revenues to the amount 
originally set as the total cost for airport operations. 
Even 
though this formula is used, there is still discretion in the 
setting of the charges and in spelling out the other terms of 
the Agreement. 
In addition to participating in the negotiations for the 
Agreement itself, the Deputy Director is responsible for annual 
modifications of the Agreement. 
These modifications to the 
charges are done pursuant to a formula in the Agreement.

CONCLUSION: Ms. Harrison can participate in decisions concerning modifications of the present Agreement. It is a more difficult question whether Ms. Harrison will be able to participate in the negotiations for a new Agreement when this Agreement expires. For each proposal being negotiated, if either the difference between the existing Agreement and the proposal or the differences between proposals on the same issue amount to a possible $100,000 effect on Western Airlines, then Ms. Harrison should not participate in negotiations on that proposal.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 February 15, 1984 J. Steven Burris Deputy County Counsel 700 H Street, Suite 2650 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-83-279 Dear Mr. Burris: This letter is sent in response to your request for advice from this office on behalf of F. Susan Harrison, the Deputy Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports, concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ FACTUAL BACKGROUND2/ Ms. Harrison's husband is employed as a pilot by Western Airlines. As a benefit of his job, Ms. Harrison and her husband receive discounted airline travel on Western and other airlines. In addition, her husband participates in an employee stock sharing program. Ms. Harrison's job duties as the Deputy Director for Finance and Administration include: 1. Preparing the Department's budget. 2. Analyzing and recommending changes to airport rates and charges (e.g., rates for T-hangars and tie-downs). _________________ 1/ California Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory references are to the California Government Code. 2/ These facts come from our telephone conversations, Ms. Harrison's memo to your office dated June 9, 1983, your letters to me dated December 13, 1983 and January 9, 1984, and the materials included with your letter. J. Steven Burris February 15, 1984 Page 2 3. Negotiating tenant leases at both Sacramento Metropolitan and Executive Airports. 4. Administering the air trade development program which is a marketing program designed to promote use of Metro Airport. My understanding is that you have already advised Ms. Harrison with respect to the setting of airport rates and charges. Your specific question to us is whether Ms. Harrison would have a conflict of interest in negotiating the airline leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport.3/ The County of Sacramento currently has an "Airline Operating Agreement and Terminal Building Lease" (the "Agreement") with the airlines for use of Sacramento Metropolitan Airport ("Metro Airport"). This Agreement was negotiated by Ms. Harrison's predecessor and became effective in June 1983; it is a five-year agreement. The negotiations for the Agreement are conducted between the Deputy Director and a group of airline representatives. The County is required to treat the airlines in an identical manner.4/ The County policy on the operation of Metro Airport is that it must be self-supporting. Accordingly, in negotiating the Agreement, the Deputy Director (Ms. Harrison) begins with the total cost of the airport operation, subtracts the amounts from revenue sources other than the airlines,5/ and arrives at the amount which must be recovered from the airlines. The airlines are charged for their use of the airport in two ways. One charge is the landing strip usage fee which is set at a certain _____________ 3/ Both of these questions raise the issue of the possible application of
embedding.summary: FPPC advises on whether Deputy Director F. Susan Harrison has a conflict of interest negotiating airline leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport given her husband's employment and stock holdings with Western Airlines. The Commission concludes she may participate in routine modifications but must disqualify herself from future negotiations on any proposals that could have a material ($100,000+) financial effect on Western Airlines.
